Analyst (Kairav Engineer) asked:
Adhesive very difficult to give volume number because there we are selling different
chemistries, some are solid, some are liquid, some are in a gel form, some are in a powder
form. Construction chemicals are there, sealants are there, additives are there. So it is very
difficult, packaging is also very different for that product. So very difficult to quantify the
volume number for that.

Analyst (Hiranand Savlani) asked:
We are expecting 15% plus full year growth for Adhesive business, 15% to 20% kind of growth.
And we have guided that we will be delivering 15% plus EBITDA margin. We are standing by
with that number. Q1 was 14% something but we are expecting that in the coming 3 quarters,
the margin improvement will be there because the GP are all-time high. That has given us the
confidence that once the value growth will come back, then the margin will also shoot up.

• On an already strong July 2020 base (+26%), adhesives grew another 36%.
• Margin outlook:
• Guided EBITDA margin of “more than 15%” for adhesives.
• Management continues to “always try to improve and increase our
margins” and cash cycle.
Capacity & Distribution
• Current adhesives plant utilization at ~40–45%, leaving ample headroom.
• Earlier reported dealer/distributor counts for adhesives will be updated with more
accurate system data in the next presentation; geography-wise split not disclosed.
Product & Chemistry
• New epoxy product Bondtite Pro launched by Resinova: described as “technical
upgraded and a revolutionary epoxy product… one of the best products in the
category so far available in India” with “exceptional acceptance.”
• R&D working to “upgrade all our chemistries” in wood, maintenance and
construction chemicals, with more new chemistries planned.
New Segments & Product Initiatives
Plastic Storage Tanks
• Launched with an Aurangabad plant; ramp-up:
• ₹1 crore/month initial run → ₹4 crore/month recently.
• Capacity expansion & geographic rollout:
• Plants operational: Aurangabad (West), Ahmedabad (West), Ghiloth (North).
• Hosur (South) under construction; targeted to be operational by November
2021.
• East to be added by year-end; management expects to cover all zones pan-
India in FY23.
• Strategic use of existing distribution:
• Will leverage ~900 piping distributors and ~33,000 dealers, many already
selling competitor tanks.

Adhesives Margin Compression (Q3)
• Key reasons:
• Sharp spike in chemical costs (both India & UK operations), with lagged pass-
through due to MRP-based pricing.
• UK subsidiary (Seal It):
• Could not obtain silicone raw material; major product is silicone-
based.
• Resulted in volume degrowth and margin pressure in Q3.
• Current status:
• Raw material prices have “softened out very fast” from the peak.
• Price hikes in adhesives have been implemented in Q4.
• UK operations: “January was excellent… because of the pent-up demand.”
• Management expects margins to rebound as RM stabilizes, but cautions that
continuous RM volatility keeps near-term margin visibility limited.
Balance Sheet, Cash & Capital Allocation
• Capex:
• “Major chunk of the expansion is going to complete this year [FY22].”
• FY23 capex will mainly be Dahej adhesives plant plus some incremental
debottlenecking.
• Debt:
• Target to be essentially net-debt free by FY24:
• “There will be no debt on the balance sheet, maybe 30 to 40 Crores
debt will be there.”
• Cash:
• Company currently sits on ₹300+ Cr cash despite high RM inventory.
• Management holding higher cash due to COVID uncertainty.
• Capital return policy (higher dividends / buyback) and future use of surplus
cash to be communicated once situation normalizes and capex for new
categories is finalized.

Analyst (Hiranand Savlani) asked:
Praveen, I think you have not seen the margin. If you see our India operation margin, it is
constantly maintained at 15%. Continuously, we are -- quarter-on-quarter, we are telling that our
margins are in the range of 15%, and we have maintained. In this year also, if you see this quarter
alone, it is 15.5%. On a half yearly basis, it is 15.7%. Last year also, you see it is 15% plus. So
we have not any diluted margin into the India operation. Whatever challenge which we have
faced, that is from the U.K. operation.
And there, we have done the correction. And you will see in the coming couple of quarters, you
will see the turnaround will start from there. But India operation is stable and continuously
giving us a growth of 15% plus. So this year also because of the extended monsoon and all, so
Q2 was a little slow. It is not for our company only. You see the adhesive other companies'
number also. Ultimately, we cannot be out of from the industry. Ultimately, whatever the
industry will perform, we can only say that our growth will be industrial, whatever growth is
there will be 1% or 2% or 3% or 5% higher than the industry growth.
We will be continuously gaining the market share. And that is what exactly we are doing into
the adhesive. You pick up the last 10-year number, not 1 quarter, 2 quarter or 1 year, pick up the
last 10-year number, we are constantly gaining the market share into the adhesive side. So that
is what our expectation. But every quarter, 15% kind of growth will be very difficult for any
company to maintain our 20% kind of. But yes, on an average, if you see, we are continuously
maintaining 15% plus growth, and that is what we have guided.
And even this difficult time also first half, we already delivered 11% -- so I don't think any way
adhesive business has any challenge. You see the compare with the peers, what the growth other
Page 19 of 21

Analyst (Rahul Agarwal) asked:
Sir three questions. Firstly, on pipes and adhesives you obviously mentioned in terms of what
margins you are looking for which are more sustainable in long term, just on the growth rates,
on volumes and revenue for either segment for pipes and adhesives. Some outlook will help for
fourth quarter of FY25-26 based on your capacity increases both in plumping as well as
adhesives, just help with some growth rates that will be helpful. That’s the first question.

Analyst (Standalone comment) asked:
“Now I think we are back to 14–15% EBITDA into the adhesive
business.”
Raw Materials & Demand
Chemical input costs corrected; “pressure on the chemicals are coming down,” leading to
improving margins from Q3 and further improvement expected from Q4 onwards.

“The way chemicals prices have started falling, it looks that 15% margin should not be the
challenge for next year.”
• Volume growth guidance: high double-digit in FY23 and 15%+ in FY24, on a higher
base.
Brand Strategy – Bondtite
• Building Bondtite as the flagship adhesive brand:
• Recently launched Bondtite Quick (Rs 5 instant joint) and Bondtite White
Glue (PVA).
• Strategy:
“We want to see that people can be connected with the Bondtite brand rather than
individual different…brand names.”
Dahej Adhesive Plant
• State-of-the-art new adhesives plant at Dahej to be operational by March 2023
(Q4).
• Will enhance capacity, production efficiency and safety.
4. Paints (Gem Paints)
Current Performance
• Q2 revenue ~Rs 50 crores (Q1 ~Rs 56 crores).
• Growth despite heavy rains in South:
“Paints business has grown by 29% in the last fiscal and a healthy EBITDA of 17%-plus.”
• Q2 EBITDA margin around 19%; H1 ~17%.
Sustainability of Margins
• Management clearly calls current margins unsustainably high:
“Because the company is small…branding cost is not allocated…these kind of margins on a
long-term basis…will not be sustainable.”
• Long-term steady-state paint margins envisaged at ~14–15%.
Integration & Strategic Plan
• Astral team has taken over finance, QC, R&D, technical/plant monitoring.
• SAP implementation underway.

• Paints: Similar synergy-led approach using adhesive network, supplemented
by new dealers.
• Ad & Brand Spend:
• Overall ad spend to remain within historical levels: “we will not be spending
extra on the newer categories.”
• Reallocation planned:
• Possibly reduce spend on existing categories and shift to new ones
once distribution and scale exist.
• Near-term: Minimal incremental ad push for new categories until distribution
and product availability are in place.
Demand Outlook & Long-Term Guidance
• Near-term:
• Q2FY23 flagged as “challenging” due to:
• Further raw material price declines (continued destocking).
• Residual inventory losses in both pipes and adhesives.
• Demand currently subdued primarily due to channel behavior, not structural
end-market weakness.
• Medium-term:
• Management is bullish on building materials over 3–4 years:
• Ready residential inventory has been liquidated.
• New residential construction activity is expected to pick up as
commodity prices fall from peaks.
• Once PVC prices stabilize, management expects volumes to “start picking up
very fast” given current “very attractive” polymer prices.
• Long-term growth guidance:
• Astral reiterated its earlier target: “We are targeting to double our top line in
the next five years on a consolidated basis.”
• They do not provide annual or segmental revenue guidance for new lines
(tanks, valves, bathware) but expect them to be key contributors to this
5-year target.

Analyst (Mr. Hiranand Savlani Speaks) asked:
ok right down the number this adhesive sales number you wanted you wanted adhesive sales number
this India operation full year number is 1098 last year was 9960 so net growth of 14.5% this quarter
adhesive number is 350 last year it was 262 so growth of 20% this quarter anything else you want
capex projection for the company so I I think will be somewhere around 250 to 300 crore max because
Kanpur is still yet to be completed because machine are yet to be installed so thats why you can
consider 300 crore max .

Analyst (Shravan Shah) asked:
Okay, got it. And second, sir, in terms of the margin in plumbing is for nine months is very good,
18.3%. Though our range was 16% to 18%. So how one can look at, is there a possibility this
margin range 16%-18% can be now on the higher side one starts building in?

Astral Limited
November 07, 2024
So we will be back not only I can say back, but will be on a higher side than what we have
guided. We have guided consolidated 15% to 16%. We are already on 16% in this quarter on a
consolidated basis. Even on a half yearly basis, if you see the number, half year basis also, we
are at 16% Pipe half year basis, 18% and paint 7.3%. India operation of adhesive on a half year
basis, it is 15.75%. So overall, you will see that we have not at all diluted any of our margin
what we have guided.
So that Sandeep bhai also mentioned there was a tremendous pressure on the polymer price,
which is reduced by almost 13.5%. In spite of that, we are in a position to maintain the margin.
There was a slight loss of inventory also in this quarter. It was not that high, but it was between
INR10 crores to INR15 crores kind of loss was there. But in spite of that, we were able to
maintain our margin.
As communicated in the past several times, our company is always believing in profitable
growth. And we have again demonstrated that in Q2, you can see our margins are maintained
within our guided range of 16% to 18% and overall 15% to 16% on a consolidated basis.
Similarly, Adhesive India, we have guided 15%, we are 15.5% on a half year basis.
As communicated Sandeep bhai in U.K., once this new R&D products are commercialized, we
are expecting a good number and improvement into the margins also. Similarly, once this U.S.
sales will pick up from Q3, some uptick will be there. But Q4, we are expecting a sizable growth
because we have launched -- we are launching in this quarter 12 products. So we are expecting
that it will be a really good number for us in the coming quarter because this is including adhesive
sealant and waterproofing all put together.
Bath ware has already started picking up. We have already reached a INR10 crores kind of
monthly run rate. So we are expecting that whatever we have guided between INR100 crores to
INR125 crores revenue for full year, we are going into the same line. Paint, we are expecting
that initial journey for launch, there will be a pressure on the margin, but we are confident that
the growth will be there for the full year. And as well as from the next year onwards, we are
expecting a reasonably high growth in the paint side.
As communicated in the last concall, we have already started controlling costs, which we
promised in the last call. And you can very well see in our numbers that our other expenses on
a consol level basis has come down from INR219 crores to INR194 crores. In percentage terms,
last quarter, it was 15.9%, which has drastically dropped to 14.2% and there are high probability
that in coming time, it will further drop because last year, there was an event of Jaipur. So
because of that, the cost was high. So we have not spent anything extra, except last quarter, there
was a back-to-back two events of cricket. And because of that, we have to spend extra spending
on the branding side.
To summarize, the margins were maintained under the difficult time. And now since
antidumping duty has initiated on PVC, we are confident that growth will come back. Also in
December, some clarity will be there on the BIS front also. Once that clarity will be there, we
will be back to our growth path of volume. Right now, volume was a little bit lower than what
Page 6 of 21

results.question: What was the impact of raw material/polymer price volatility, and what inventory loss/gain was reported?
results.expected_answer: Sandeep Engineer (Q2 FY25 call): 'PVC prices went down by 13.5% during a single quarter... from beginning of November the prices have started an uptrend... due to this antidumping duty in place, the PVC prices went up by INR1 on 1st of November and again by INR2 on 7th of November.' Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, on the same anti-dumping duty context): 'Already INR3 price rise is taken place. We are expecting between INR6 to INR8 price rise should happen over a period of time.' Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet, FY25 annual): 'last year alone, the polymer price was down by 18%. Despite a 18% fall... Astral is the only company in the country that has improved its gross profit margin. We have improved the GP by 1%.' Inventory loss/gain impact was also quantified: Hiranand Savlani (Q2 call): 'a slight loss of inventory also in this quarter... between INR10 crores to INR15 crores kind of loss.'
results.answer:
## Summary  
The impact of raw material and polymer price volatility led to fluctuations in costs, affecting profitability and causing inventory losses due to price changes. Specifically, inventory losses were reported due to fluctuations in raw material and chemical prices.

## Details  
- The FY23 Annual Report (Page 23) states that the decrease in return on net worth was mainly due to the impact of business combination amortization and inventory losses caused by fluctuations in raw material and chemical prices.  
- The FY24 Annual Report (Page 17) highlights raw material price volatility, particularly polymers and chemicals, as a risk affecting profitability. The company mitigates this through strategic sourcing, long-term contracts, backward integration, and price adjustments.  
- The FY23 Annual Report (Page 12) also mentions foreign exchange fluctuations impacting raw material prices, contributing to price volatility risks.  
- No specific numeric figure for the inventory loss amount is provided in the retrieved context.

## Sources  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2023_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 23 · FY23  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 17 · FY24  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2023_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 12 · FY23

results.question: What are the capital expenditure (capex) plans and guidance for the current and next fiscal year?
results.expected_answer: Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, in response to Rahul Agarwal): 'I think we should be closing around Rs. 450 crore or so for the full year' (FY25) and for the following year, 'Next year should be somewhere around 250 or so' (FY26), explained by Sandeep Engineer as being lower because 'Kanpur is almost completed' and no major new facility additions are planned, with spending mainly on machinery rather than land/buildings. In the Q2 FY25 call, capex for H1 FY25 was stated as INR282 crores with full-year capex expected to be 'the same line only... now balance will be more than INR100 crores' with Kanpur cited as a driver. In the Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani separately noted 'In the last two years alone, we have pumped in close to ₹1000 crore into this expansion activity, particularly capex,' with FY26 capex guided at '250 to 300 crore max because Kanpur is still yet to be completed.'
results.answer:
## Summary  
The capital expenditure (CapEx) plan for FY24 is approximately ₹350 crore, with about ₹300 crore already spent in the first half and around ₹50 crore planned for the second half. For FY25 and FY26, the company has indicated a continuation of CapEx with a rough budget of ₹300 crore per year, primarily focused on pipe capacity expansion and other business segments, with flexibility to increase depending on demand.

## Details  
- FY24 CapEx guidance is ₹350 crore total. As of Q2 FY24, nearly ₹300 crore was already spent, leaving about ₹50 crore for H2 FY24 (AstralConcall24Q2.pdf · Page 9 · FY24).  
- An additional ₹70-75 crore CapEx was incurred in FY24 for land acquisition near the corporate house, which was not originally planned (AstralConcall24Q2.pdf · Page 9 · FY24).  
- The CapEx plan includes phased capacity expansions: Hyderabad (₹40 crore + ₹30 crore) and Kanpur (₹30 crore + ₹30 crore), totaling around ₹130 crore for these two locations (AstralConcall24Q4.pdf · Page 20 · FY24).  
- Approximately ₹250 crore of the FY24 CapEx is allocated to pipe business, with ₹50 crore for other businesses (AstralConcall24Q4.pdf · Page 20 · FY24).  
- For FY25 and FY26, the company indicated a CapEx budget roughly around ₹300 crore per year, with potential to increase if demand is robust (AstralConcall25Q3.pdf · Page 11 · FY25; AstralConcall24Q4.pdf · Page 20 · FY24).  
- Earlier years’ CapEx included ₹250 crore in FY22 and ₹200 crore planned over FY23–FY24 for pipes and adhesives, indicating a steady investment trend (AstralConcall22Q2.pdf · Page 4 · FY22).

## Sources  
AstralConcall24Q2.pdf · Page 9 · FY24  
AstralConcall24Q4.pdf · Page 20 · FY24  
AstralConcall25Q3.pdf · Page 11 · FY25  
AstralConcall22Q2.pdf · Page 4 · FY22

results.question: What capacity additions or plant updates did management detail?
results.expected_answer: Specific figures given in the Q3 FY25 call (Hiranand Savlani, in response to Keshav Lahoti): 'This year we have already added 47,000 metric tons in 9 month basis' of pipe capacity; Kanpur plant total capacity will be '60,000' metric tons 'in two phases. First will be 30 and then the second will be another 30' with '30 will be coming to FY26 and 30 will be coming to FY27'; Hyderabad plant total planned capacity was '70,000,' of which 'we already completed I think 25,000 metric tonne.' Separately, in the Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani stated capacity has grown at a '10.3% CAGR, while sales are happening at a 13.5% CAGR,' with utilization 'in the 55 to 65 range.' In the Q3 call it was also stated overall capacity increased 'by 36,000 metric tons from 334k to 370k metric tons' during the year.
results.answer:
## Summary  
Management detailed multiple capacity additions and plant updates including the near completion of the Guwahati leased plant (22,000 MT), internal plant expansions adding 20,000 MT, potential further additions of 10,000-15,000 MT by adding machines, and the upcoming Telangana plant expected to be operational by Q2 FY25. Additionally, a sizable capacity addition of 30,000 MT is expected next year from the Kanpur plant, with a total capacity increase of 40,000-45,000 MT anticipated in FY25.

## Details  
- Guwahati leased plant: ~22,000 MT capacity, almost complete, with tank machines already rolled out and pipes to follow (Q2 FY24 concall).  
- Internal plants: Recently added 20,000 MT capacity by debottlenecking and machine additions (Q2 FY24 concall).  
- Additional capacity potential: 10,000-15,000 MT can be added by installing more machines in existing plants (Q2 FY24 concall).  
- Telangana plant: Expected to be operational in Q2 FY25, adding new capacity (Q2 FY24 concall).  
- Kanpur plant: Expected to add around 30,000 MT capacity once operational next year (Q3 FY25 concall).  
- Total capacity addition expected in FY25: 40,000-45,000 MT including Kanpur and other plant corrections (Q3 FY25 concall).  
- Existing plants have spare land and capacity for further expansion, including Dholka, Santej, and Ghiloth plants, with a new state-of-the-art fittings plant nearing completion at Ghiloth (Q2 FY24 concall).  
- Management is continuously monitoring market conditions to time capacity additions appropriately and is confident about cash flow to support expansions (Q1 and Q2 FY24 concalls).  

## Sources  
AstralConcall24Q1.pdf · Pages 7, 18 · FY24  
AstralConcall24Q2.pdf · Pages 8, 23 · FY24  
AstralConcall25Q3.pdf · Page 13 · FY25

results.question: What updates did management provide regarding new acquisitions, overseas offices, or paints/adhesives expansion?
results.expected_answer: Management discussed the acquisition of Al Aziz (Sandeep Engineer, Analyst Meet): rationale was to enter the PE/PP pipe and fittings product line, including German-approved gas fittings and water-line fittings; 'Now we are getting four machines—three are coming, and one more will come later—for making PE and PP pipes in our plants.' No specific investment amount or timeline in months/years was disclosed for this acquisition. The company also opened its 'first overseas office in Dubai' (Hiranand Savlani, Analyst Meet) targeting Middle East/Gulf/Africa exports - no investment figure given. On new geography for Paint, Sandeep Engineer/Hiranand Savlani (Q2 FY25 call) discussed phased launches: Astral Paint launched in Gujarat, then Rajasthan/Jaipur, with plans to enter 'Maharashtra and MP in next 5 to 6 months' - qualitative timeline given but no specific investment figure. UK adhesive technology/backward integration deal was also mentioned (Hiranand Savlani, Q3 call): 'we have just signed the agreement. So in the Q1, that will be available to us' - no investment amount disclosed.
results.answer:
## Summary  
Management provided updates on significant capacity expansion in adhesives, including a new Dahej plant expected to increase adhesive business revenue from ~₹1,000 crore to close to ₹1,800 crore. They highlighted ongoing product portfolio completion in adhesives with focus on newer applications and category expansion. The paints business completed its first full year under the Astral Coatings brand, with plans to grow the paints segment to 15-20% of revenue in the coming years. Overseas operations in the UK and US are active with R&D collaboration and product launches, including water-based waterproofing products being introduced in India. No explicit mention of new acquisitions or overseas office openings was provided in the retrieved context.

## Details  

- **Adhesives Expansion:**  
  - Dahej plant capacity addition expected to raise adhesive revenue from ~₹1,000 crore last year to about ₹1,800 crore (AstralConcall23Q3.pdf · Page 4 · FY23).  
  - Management stated the adhesive product range is now complete across wood working adhesives, maintenance adhesives, sealants, and tapes, with ongoing efforts to explore newer applications and category expansions (AstralConcall25Q4.pdf · Page 10 · FY25).  
  - Adhesives business expected to grow steadily, supported by capacity enhancements and product diversification (Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 21 · FY25).  

- **Paints Business:**  
  - Paints business completed its first full year under the Astral Coatings identity (Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 21 · FY25).  
  - Management targets paints to contribute 15-20% of revenue in the next few years, driven by quality, innovation, and eco-friendly formulations (Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 24 · FY24).  
  - Paints growth in 9M FY23 was ~15% (AstralConcall23Q3.pdf · Page 4 · FY23).  

- **Overseas Operations and R&D:**  
  - UK and US units have strong R&D collaboration with India, working on advanced chemistries including single component and water-based products for waterproofing (AstralConcall23Q4.pdf · Page 15 · FY24).  
  - Water-based waterproofing products developed in UK and US are being launched in India (AstralConcall23Q4.pdf · Page 15 · FY24).  
  - UK business is expected to see gradual recovery with focus on export-led growth, especially in tapes category (Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 21 · FY25).  

- **Acquisitions and Overseas Offices:**  
  - No explicit updates on new acquisitions or opening of overseas offices were found in the retrieved context.  

## Sources  
AstralConcall23Q3.pdf · Page 4 · FY23  
AstralConcall25Q4.pdf · Page 10 · FY25  
AstralConcall23Q4.pdf · Page 15 · FY24  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 24 · FY24  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 21 · FY25

Capacity & Distribution

• Earlier reported dealer/distributor counts for adhesives will be updated with more

Product & Chemistry

upgraded and a revolutionary epoxy product… one of the best products in the
category so far available in India” with “exceptional acceptance.”
• R&D working to “upgrade all our chemistries” in wood, maintenance and
construction chemicals, with more new chemistries planned.
New Segments & Product Initiatives
Plastic Storage Tanks
• Launched with an Aurangabad plant; ramp-up:

• Capacity expansion & geographic rollout:

• East to be added by year-end; management expects to cover all zones pan-
India in FY23.
• Strategic use of existing distribution:
• Will leverage ~900 piping distributors and ~33,000 dealers, many already
selling competitor tanks.

Analyst (Attendee) asked:
Thanks for that. Second and final question. When we started our adhesive business, I remember you
had said that we have entered to out of eight or two out of nine segments if I remember correctly five
six seven years ago. What is your view now? Are we ready to enter something more of the remaining

• Paints: Similar synergy-led approach using adhesive network, supplemented
by new dealers.
• Ad & Brand Spend:
• Overall ad spend to remain within historical levels: “we will not be spending
extra on the newer categories.”
• Reallocation planned:
• Possibly reduce spend on existing categories and shift to new ones
once distribution and scale exist.
• Near-term: Minimal incremental ad push for new categories until distribution
and product availability are in place.
Demand Outlook & Long-Term Guidance
• Near-term:
• Q2FY23 flagged as “challenging” due to:
• Further raw material price declines (continued destocking).
• Residual inventory losses in both pipes and adhesives.
• Demand currently subdued primarily due to channel behavior, not structural
end-market weakness.
• Medium-term:
• Management is bullish on building materials over 3–4 years:
• Ready residential inventory has been liquidated.
• New residential construction activity is expected to pick up as
commodity prices fall from peaks.
• Once PVC prices stabilize, management expects volumes to “start picking up
very fast” given current “very attractive” polymer prices.
• Long-term growth guidance:
• Astral reiterated its earlier target: “We are targeting to double our top line in
the next five years on a consolidated basis.”
• They do not provide annual or segmental revenue guidance for new lines
(tanks, valves, bathware) but expect them to be key contributors to this
5-year target.

Analyst (Mr. Sandeep Engineer) asked:
Hello. In Adhesives there are four segments and the first segment which is called maintenance has
certain chemistries. Segments are based on where the application is and with the chemistry. So
epoxies, sinoacrylates and silicones and many such products fall under the category of maintenance,
where maintenance is called a category used in construction. But it's joining the stone or many other
such applications.
The wood is mainly for the wood joinings. The construction chemicals which is the fastest growing
category of the Adhesives has many applications. Cementation mixtures, then waterproofing, then a
lot of things connected with fast setting of cements, fast setting where you can speed up the
construction work. So today globally also construction chemicals is the fastest growing segment and
India is much behind the globe because of, Here there are many reasons this segment got not covered
and there were many reasons which you would not like to discuss here but today if you see with more
companies, with branded companies coming into construction chemicals, also paint companies
coming into construction chemicals, this segment is going to grow at a fast pace and will be mainly
used for, many applications where the raw construction is done, waterproofing and cementing and
mixtures. And industrial is the same Products when sold in bulk for industry is industrial segment.
Products are same but if you sell 500 grams to 1 kg, 2 kg is normal retail. If you go to industry, you go
to Agra and sell Epoxy for making bangles is an industrial sense. So all these are different four
segments in Adhesives, and each segment, as far as I am understanding from the market, has picked
up a huge growth.
There are certain chemistries and certain products which have come in the international market,
especially Europe and America and many other markets. We are still much behind in that and when
these hybrids, these are called hybrids, Single component. Now there are a lot of two component
products. India is on two component products. The world is moving to single component product. And
as it moves to single component product, the whole way of looking at Adhesives will change and we
in our UK and US plant are moving to single component. We have water based products in UK and US,
which we are launching in India for doing waterproofing. So these are also good, in way of not creating
hazard environmentally and for the applicator. So a lot of things are changing.
The good part we have, we have a UK unit, we have a US unit where the R&D is strong, and the India
unit where even the R&D is strong. So everyone talks to each other. A lot of development is done in
between all these units and we have access to chemistries from global countries like UK and US. A lot
of these things are helping us. There are certain things we are working on very advanced chemistries.
It's too early to communicate on that till we don't bring it to the market and try selling it. But we are
already going to launch it at some point of time this year.

results.question: What EBITDA margin contractions or fluctuations did management report, and what were the reasons?
results.expected_answer: Quantified margin impacts disclosed in the Q2 FY25 call (Hiranand Savlani): consolidated EBITDA fell from 17% (last year) to '15.97%, so precisely 16%,' described as 'a 1% drop on the EBITDA'; consolidated EBITDA in absolute terms fell from 'INR233 crores versus this year, INR219 crores, so almost 6% negative EBITDA growth'; UK adhesive EBITDA swung from '9.7% EBITDA' last year to '2% EBITDA' this quarter, and in a later reference from 'INR9.1 crores' to 'a negative EBITDA of minus INR2 crores'; polymer/PVC price fell 'by almost 13.5%' during the quarter; inventory loss was quantified at 'between INR10 crores to INR15 crores.' In the Q3 FY25 call, pipe EBITDA was maintained/grown, with Sandeep Engineer citing 10.9% EBITDA growth for the pipe vertical despite polymer price volatility, and Analyst Meet figures show GP margin improved 'by 1%' despite an 18% polymer price fall for FY25.
results.answer:
## Summary
Management reported EBITDA margin fluctuations primarily due to inventory gains and losses, business expansion costs, and integration of acquisitions. They noted unsustainable peak margins driven by inventory gains, margin dilution from new business launches and branding spends, and expected margin improvements post these phases.

## Details

- **Inventory Effects on Margins (FY22–FY23):**  
  Management stated that recent EBITDA margins peaked at 20–21% due to inventory gains but considered these margins unsustainable. The sustainable EBITDA margin range was indicated as 15–17%. In FY22 Q4, inventory losses due to polymer price corrections brought margins back to the normalized 16–18% range.  
  (Source: AstralConcall23Q2.pdf · Page 3 · FY23; AstralConcall22Q4.pdf · Page 8 · FY22)

- **Business Expansion and Branding Costs (FY25):**  
  Management highlighted that ongoing launches and expansion efforts, including adding teams, dealer distribution, and heavy branding spends, caused lower EBITDA margins due to a low base effect. They expect margin improvement from the second half of FY25 onwards once these investments stabilize.  
  (Source: AstralConcall25Q3.pdf · Page 15 · FY25)

- **Acquisitions and Integration Impact:**  
  In a query about acquisitions' impact on margins over the next ten years, management did not provide a definitive margin accretive or dilutive outcome in the retrieved context, so no explicit conclusion can be drawn.  
  (Source: AstralConcall23Q4.pdf · Page 26 · FY24)

- **Adhesives Segment Margins:**  
  Adhesives segment EBITDA margin was 13.4% in Q2 FY23 and 14.4% in H1 FY23, with management expecting improvement to 15%+ EBITDA margin from FY24 due to falling raw material costs.  
  (Source: AstralConcall23Q2.pdf · Page 3 · FY23; AstralConcall24Q1.pdf · Page 6 · FY24)

- **Plumbing Business Margins:**  
  Plumbing business delivered EBITDA margins of 16% in Q3 FY24 and 17% on a nine-month basis, within the guided range of 15–17%, despite some one-off expenses.  
  (Source: AstralConcall24Q3.pdf · Page 2 · FY24)

results.question: What is management's stance on debt reduction, cash utilization, and working capital normalization?
results.expected_answer: No specific debt reduction target was disclosed in any of the three transcripts; Astral is described as net cash positive rather than carrying debt to reduce. Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet): 'Right now, we are sitting on a net cash of ₹464 crore.' On cash utilization/dividend, in the Q2 FY25 call, Pinaki Banerjee asked how the company plans to utilize its cash of 'around Rs. 290 crores'; Hiranand Savlani responded: 'I think by year end we will be able to know what is our next year budget. Based on that we will work out our cash utilization plan and if we don't have any such kind of utilization plan then we will give it to our shareholders' - no specific dividend payout ratio or percentage was committed. On working capital, Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet) noted creditor/debtor/inventory day movements tied to a one-off rupee-appreciation-driven early creditor payoff in March, but gave no specific working-capital-day normalization target or timeline in days.
results.answer:
## Summary  
Management indicated that debt reduction and cash utilization plans will be finalized after the budget is set by year-end, and if there is no specific utilization plan, surplus cash will be returned to shareholders. Working capital normalization is underway, with temporary increases explained by creditor payoffs, and recent trends show improvement.

## Details  

- **Debt Reduction and Cash Utilization:**  
  From the Q3 FY25 earnings call transcript (AstralConcall25Q3.pdf · Page 22), management stated:  
  > "By year end we will be able to know what is our next year budget. Based on that we will work out our cash utilization plan and if we don't have any such kind of utilization plan then we will give it to our shareholders."  
  This indicates a cautious and planned approach to cash deployment, prioritizing budget-driven decisions and shareholder returns if no better use is identified.

- **Working Capital Normalization:**  
  From the Q4 FY25 earnings call transcript (AstralConcall25Q4.pdf · Page 5), management explained:  
  > "This is the plumbing business working capital cycle. A similar graph, mainly because the creditor payoff taken place in the plumbing business only. Because of that, you’re seeing an increase in net working capital days. But that has come down again in the month of May. Nothing to worry about on that side it was about taking advantage of the currency."  
  This shows that the working capital increase was temporary and related to creditor payments, with normalization already occurring.

## Sources  
AstralConcall25Q3.pdf · Page 22 · FY25  
AstralConcall25Q4.pdf · Page 5 · FY25

last year’s first quarter, the volume growth across industries was very good. We were also close to 16%
volume growth, and then it started a southward journey. This is a continuous falling graph, and last
year alone, the polymer price was down by 18%. Despite a 18% fall, you can see that in this
environment, Astral is the only company in the country that has improved its gross profit margin. We
have improved the GP by 1%, and we have maintained our EBITDA.
So this is the power of the brand. I can say that we have established ourselves in the market, where
people respect the brand, and people are ready to pay us for the quality. Many players have tried a lot
of things by undercutting the value and throwing the material in the market with material, but even
after that, they were not able to grow their volumes. So, customers understand that quality players
will always command a premium, and that’s what we are always communicating that we believe in
consistency. We don’t want zigzag growth. We want to maintain consistency in terms of growth and
margins as well. And that’s what we are doing right now. There were a lot of pressures on us too, with
competitors selling at lower prices, and now they are all suffering. Many of their projects are getting
blacklisted as well. We don’t want to name anyone, but everyone has started facing this problem in
the market.
This is a graph of paint and adhesive. You can see the last five years' growth; you can see 21.23% but
that’s not the correct graph because paint was added at a later stage. So, it may not be the right graph.
The EBITDA growth is 8.55%, and the profit before tax (PBT) is low because we have taken a lot of
amortizations that’s why it is showing very low Over the last three years, if you see, we have made a
lot of corrections. New additions are taking place for dealers, and because of that, EBITDA is under
pressure, particularly in the paint business. But now, I think, once our volumes start picking up from
this year itself, you will see a lot of improvement in this as well.
As far as the adhesive business is concerned, I think we are consistently growing at a 17 t0 18 % for
the last 11 years from where we have started our journey in 2014 and when we started that time our
EBITDA percentage was just 6% now from 6% journey to this year full year number we are close to
17% I am not talking about the last quarter where we had a closed to about 19% EBITDA but that was
was the peak quarter so because of that it was high but otherwise on a full year there wages also we
were close to about 17%. So adhesive business of India is continuously doing well. Even UK was also
doing great for us because we are having access to the new technology from the UK and we are getting
lot of inputs from our UK unit. It is unfortunate that the last year was the abnormal year for the UK
company. Otherwise last 10 year we have seen a good number. We started journey from almost ₹70-
80 crore top line in UK. And from 80 crore top line to this year we close with 346 so almost you can say
3X more than 3X in the span of just an year. So UK has also deliver a good number. This was the only
exceptional year and we are confident that we will be delivering the good number from the UK also
from the current year itself.
So, this is a very small letter. So we are going to share this presentation to you. So you can go through
that also. So what is the industry outlook? What we are saying? So we we are of the view that the

Analyst (Hiranand Savlani) asked:
Yes. And it is up to us how fast we want to ramp up. If we really see that the demand is exceeding
what we are planning, then we can do it a little early also because cash is not a problem to the
organization. We can deploy a fast track team and we can try to complete the expansion activity
early also. It depends how the demand is going to work out because in the last 2 quarters at least,

results.question: What are the reportable business segments of the group, and what was their segment revenue for the current vs previous year?
results.expected_answer: Astral Limited (Group) has two reportable business segments — 'Plumbing' and 'Paints and Adhesives'. Segment revenue for FY25 (year ended March 31, 2025) vs FY24: Plumbing — ₹41,963 Million (FY24: ₹41,420 Million); Paints and Adhesives — ₹16,361 Million (FY24: ₹14,994 Million); Total segment revenue — ₹58,324 Million (FY24: ₹56,414 Million). Note: this segment disclosure is presented only in the Consolidated Financial Statements; the Standalone Financial Statements (Note 41) state that no segment disclosure is given standalone since it is presented in the consolidated statements (Ind AS 108).
results.answer:
## Summary
The group has two reportable business segments: **Plumbing** and **Paints and Adhesives**. For the year ended March 31, 2024, segment revenues were ₹4,142 crore for Plumbing and ₹1,499 crore for Paints and Adhesives. For the previous year ended March 31, 2023, segment revenues were ₹3,767 crore for Plumbing and ₹1,391 crore for Paints and Adhesives.

## Details
- Year ended March 31, 2024:
  - Plumbing segment revenue: ₹41,420 million (₹4,142 crore)
  - Paints and Adhesives segment revenue: ₹14,994 million (₹1,499 crore)
  - Total segment revenue: ₹56,414 million (₹5,641 crore)

- Year ended March 31, 2023:
  - Plumbing segment revenue: ₹37,675 million (₹3,767 crore)
  - Paints and Adhesives segment revenue: ₹13,910 million (₹1,391 crore)
  - Total segment revenue: ₹51,585 million (₹5,159 crore)

results.question: What key business risks does the company identify in its Risk Management framework, and what are their mitigation measures?
results.expected_answer: Not disclosed as a single itemized list in that exact section beyond the categories shown in the 'Risk Management' framework: Market Competition; Technological Relevance; Environmental and Social Responsibility; Talent Acquisition and Retention; Supply Chain Continuity; Product Quality and Safety; Intellectual Property Protection; Compliance and Regulatory Adherence. For each, a corresponding mitigation measure is listed (e.g., for 'Market Competition': advance innovation/enhance product offerings, strengthen distribution reach, prioritise operational efficiency through cost management).
results.answer:
## Summary  
The company identifies key business risks including market competition, raw material price volatility, currency risk, interest rate risk, credit risk, and liquidity risk. Mitigation measures include continuous innovation, expanding distribution, cost optimization, strategic sourcing, long-term supplier contracts, backward integration, monitoring market trends, use of derivative instruments for financial risks, and robust internal controls with regular audit oversight.

## Details  

### Key Business Risks Identified  
- **Market Competition Risk:** Intense competition from existing and new players impacting market share and profitability.  
- **Raw Material Price Volatility:** Fluctuations in prices of polymers and chemicals affecting profitability.  
- **Currency Risk:** Exposure to foreign exchange fluctuations due to international operations.  
- **Interest Rate Risk:** Exposure arising from borrowings and financial instruments.  
- **Credit Risk:** Risk of counterparty default on trade receivables and financial transactions.  
- **Liquidity Risk:** Risk related to the company’s ability to meet its financial obligations timely.  

### Mitigation Measures  
- **For Market Competition:**  
  - Continuous innovation and product development to maintain competitive edge.  
  - Expansion of distribution network and strengthening brand presence.  
  - Emphasis on cost optimization and operational efficiency.  

- **For Raw Material Price Volatility:**  
  - Strategic sourcing and long-term contracts with suppliers.  
  - Backward integration and captive manufacturing of key raw materials.  
  - Continuous monitoring of market trends and timely price adjustments.  

- **For Currency and Interest Rate Risks:**  
  - Use of derivative financial instruments aligned with underlying exposures to hedge currency and interest rate risks.  

- **For Credit Risk:**  
  - Monitoring and managing credit exposure from trade receivables and financial assets.  

- **For Liquidity Risk:**  
  - Maintaining appropriate risk limits and controls, with periodic reviews and internal audit oversight.  

- **Governance and Monitoring:**  
  - Risk management policies are periodically reviewed and approved by the Audit Committee.  
  - Internal audit conducts regular and ad hoc reviews of risk management controls and reports to the Audit Committee.  
  - A risk management committee oversees the development and implementation of risk mitigation strategies.  

## Sources

results.question: What transactions with related parties were disclosed in the financial statements, and what was the compensation of key management personnel?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 37 (Related Party Disclosures) discloses transactions with Subsidiaries, Joint Venture, Enterprises over which KMP have significant influence, KMP, and Relatives of KMP for FY25 vs FY24 (₹ in Million), including: CSR Expenditure – Astral Foundation 126 (108); Interest Income – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 43 (19); Investment in Subsidiaries – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (2,773); Redemption of debentures – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (1,940); Purchase of Goods/Services – Kairamya Journeys LLP 141 (130); Reimbursement of expenses claimed – Astral Coatings Private Limited 60 (49); Remuneration – Sandeep Engineer 152 (139), Hiranand Savlani 59 (42), Others 57 (52); Rent Paid – Jagruti Sandeep Engineer 1 (1), Sandeep Engineer HUF 1 (1); Sale of Goods – Astral Coatings Private Limited 19 (19); Sitting Fees to Independent Directors (0–1 each); Loans Given – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 477 (91); Loans Repaid – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (8); year-end balances: Loans Given outstanding – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 876 (377); Interest accrued on loan – 20 (5); Payables – Sandeep Engineer 47 (45), Others 5 (4); Advance for purchase of non-current investment – Astral Pipes Limited 109 (109). Compensation of Key Management Personnel (short-term benefits): ₹251 Million (FY24: ₹216 Million).
results.answer:
## Summary  
The financial statements disclose that related party transactions were conducted in the ordinary course of business and on an arm’s length basis, with no materially significant transactions conflicting with the company’s interests. The compensation of key management personnel primarily consisted of short-term benefits and sitting fees, including employer contributions to provident fund but excluding provisions for gratuity and leave encashment.

## Details  

### Related Party Transactions  
- All related party transactions during the financial years under review (FY22, FY23, FY24, FY25) were in the ordinary course of business and on an arm’s length basis.  
- There were no materially significant transactions with related parties that conflicted with the interests of the company.  
- Transactions were placed before the Audit Committee and the Board for approval, with prior omnibus approval obtained for foreseen and repetitive transactions. These transactions were reviewed quarterly by the Audit Committee and Board.  
- Information in Form AOC-2 was not annexed due to the absence of materially significant conflicting transactions.  
- Related parties include subsidiaries, joint ventures, enterprises under significant influence of key managerial personnel, key managerial personnel themselves, and their relatives.  
- Sales to and purchases from related parties were made on terms equivalent to those prevailing in arm’s length transactions.  
- Outstanding balances with related parties were unsecured and settled in cash.  
- The policy on related party transactions is publicly available on the company’s website.

### Compensation of Key Management Personnel  
- Compensation includes:  
  - Short-term benefits (salary, allowances, etc.)  
  - Sitting fees  
  - Employer contribution to provident fund  
- Excludes:  
  - Employees’ stock options  
  - Provisions for gratuity and leave encashment (based on actuarial valuation for the company as a whole, not individually ascertainable)  
- The remuneration is determined by the Remuneration Committee and approved by the Board.  
- No exact numeric figures for total compensation are provided in the retrieved context.

results.question: What operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions did the company detail in the annual report?
results.expected_answer: Directors' Report states that during FY25 the Company increased its installed capacity of the plumbing business by 14.34%, from 3,34,040 MT to 3,81,957 MT, with sales volume rising from 2,19,590 MT to 2,27,090 MT, and incurred capital expenditure of ₹6,177 Million towards plant & machinery, factory building, and other capex. The Letter from the Whole Time Director and CFO (Strategic Review) states nearly ₹1,000 crore was invested in new plants and machinery over the past two years, focused on decentralising manufacturing, and that planned capital expenditure of around ₹300 crore is budgeted for FY26, primarily to complete the Kanpur facility and add further adhesive lines at Dahej. Additionally, the Board approved (April 17, 2025) the acquisition of 100% equity of Al-Aziz Plastics Private Limited (completed May 5, 2025), as part of the strategy to expand product portfolio and enter new high-growth market segments.
results.answer:
## Summary
The company increased its installed capacity by 6.54% from 257,946 MT to 274,822 MT in FY22 and by 14.34% from 334,040 MT to 381,957 MT in FY25. Capital expenditures were ₹2,151 million in FY22 and ₹6,177 million in FY25 towards plant, machinery, factory building, and other assets. Additionally, the company pursued strategic acquisitions as part of its growth strategy in FY25.

## Details
- **Installed Capacity Expansion:**
  - FY22: Increased from 257,946 MT to 274,822 MT (6.54% increase) [Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2022_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 27 · FY22]
  - FY25: Plumbing business capacity increased from 334,040 MT to 381,957 MT (14.34% increase) [Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 26 · FY25]

- **Sales Volume Increase:**
  - FY22: Sales volume increased from 136,593 MT to 149,569 MT [Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2022_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 27 · FY22]
  - FY25: Sales volume increased from 219,590 MT to 227,090 MT [Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 26 · FY25]

- **Capital Expenditure:**
  - FY22: ₹2,151 million spent on plant & machinery, factory building, and other capital expenditure [Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2022_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 27 · FY22]
  - FY25: ₹6,177 million spent on plant & machinery, factory building, and other capital expenditure [Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 26 · FY25]

- **Strategic Acquisitions:**
  - FY25: The company undertook acquisitions to expand its product portfolio and enter new high-growth markets as part of its strategy [Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 26 · FY25]

## Sources
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2022_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 27 · FY22  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 26 · FY25

results.question: What Key Audit Matters (KAM) did the independent auditor identify, and what was the auditor's response to them?
results.expected_answer: The Independent Auditor's Report (Standalone) identifies two Key Audit Matters: (1) Impairment assessment of Goodwill (Note 2(x)(iv)) — Goodwill of ₹2,036 Million as at March 31, 2025, tested annually via discounted cash-flow models of Cash Generating Units (CGUs); flagged as a KAM due to judgmental assumptions on projected revenue growth, operating margins, cash flows, and discount rates. Auditor's response: assessed CGU definitions for compliance, evaluated management's future cash flow forecasts, compared forecasts to actual results, focused on sales growth/discount rate assumptions, recalculated estimates, involved valuation specialists, and assessed disclosures. (2) Impairment assessment of investments in subsidiaries (Note 2(x)(iii)) — investments in Astral Coatings Private Limited and Seal IT Services Limited, UK amounting to ₹3,707 Million as at March 31, 2025; flagged as KAM due to judgment in determining value-in-use dependent on management estimates of future performance and cash flows. Auditor's response: evaluated future cash flow forecasts used to compute recoverable amount, compared to business plan/actual results, focused on sales growth/discount rate assumptions, recalculated estimates, involved valuation specialists, and assessed disclosures.
results.answer:
## Summary  
The independent auditor identified certain Key Audit Matters (KAM) for Astral Limited’s standalone financial statements for FY25, which were considered most significant in the audit. The auditor responded by performing specific audit procedures designed to address the risks of material misstatement related to these matters, providing a sufficient and appropriate basis for their audit opinion.

## Details  
- The auditor determined the KAMs based on professional judgment as those matters of most significance in the audit of the standalone financial statements for the year ended March 31, 2025.  
- The audit included procedures specifically designed to respond to the assessed risks of material misstatement associated with these KAMs.  
- The results of these audit procedures, including those addressing the KAMs, formed the basis for the auditor’s opinion on the standalone financial statements.  
- The auditor communicated these KAMs to those charged with governance and described how the audit addressed each matter in the auditor’s report.  
- The auditor did not provide a separate opinion on the KAMs themselves but addressed them in the context of the overall audit opinion.  
- The auditor also confirmed compliance with ethical requirements regarding independence and communicated any relationships or matters that could bear on independence, applying safeguards where applicable.

The exact nature or description of the individual KAMs and the detailed audit procedures performed to address each are not explicitly stated in the retrieved context.

## Sources  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 97 · FY25  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 99 · FY25

results.expected_answer: Consolidated Financial Statements, Note 37 (Segment Reporting) — Segment Profit for year ended March 31, 2025 (FY24 in brackets): Plumbing ₹6,126 Million (₹6,054 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹1,150 Million (₹1,396 Million); Total ₹7,276 Million (₹7,450 Million). Segment Assets as at March 31, 2025 (March 31, 2024): Plumbing ₹28,677 Million (₹24,623 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹15,603 Million (₹14,222 Million); Total Segment Assets ₹44,280 Million (₹38,845 Million). Segment Liabilities as at March 31, 2025 (March 31, 2024): Plumbing ₹7,521 Million (₹7,657 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹3,432 Million (₹2,606 Million); Total Segment Liabilities ₹10,953 Million (₹10,263 Million). results.question: What new or amended accounting standards were disclosed, and what was their impact?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 2(y) 'New and amended standards' discloses: (1) Ind AS 117 'Insurance Contracts' — notified by MCA on August 12, 2024 under the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Amendment Rules, 2024, effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after April 1, 2024. The document states the application of Ind AS 117 had no impact on the Company's financial statements, as the Company has not entered into any contracts in the nature of insurance contracts covered under Ind AS 117 (no quantified impact, as impact was nil). (2) Amendment to Ind AS 116 'Leases' — Lease Liability in a Sale and Leaseback, notified via the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Second Amendment Rules, 2024, effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after April 1, 2024. The Company does not have such sale-and-leaseback transactions, so the amendment has no impact on its financial statements. Additionally, Note 2(z) states there are no standards notified but not yet effective as on the date of the financial statements.
results.answer:
## Summary  
The company disclosed amendments to Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) 8, 1, and 12 effective from April 1, 2023. These amendments clarify the definition of accounting estimates, require disclosure of material accounting policies instead of significant ones, and narrow the scope of the initial recognition exception for deferred tax related to assets and liabilities from a single transaction. The company stated these amendments had no impact on the measurement, recognition, presentation, or financial statements.

## Details  
- **Amendments to Ind AS 8 (Definition of Accounting Estimates):**  
  Clarifies the distinction between changes in accounting estimates, changes in accounting policies, and correction of errors. It also clarifies how entities use measurement techniques and inputs to develop accounting estimates.  
  *Impact:* No material impact expected on the financial statements.  
  (Sources: Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2023_AnnualReport.pdf Pages 113, 174, 175; Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf Page 129)

- **Amendments to Ind AS 1 (Disclosure of Accounting Policies):**  
  Replaces the requirement to disclose ‘significant’ accounting policies with a requirement to disclose ‘material’ accounting policies, adding guidance on applying materiality in policy disclosures.  
  *Impact:* No impact on measurement or recognition; the company is revisiting accounting policies to comply with the amended requirements.  
  (Sources: Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2023_AnnualReport.pdf Pages 175, 194; Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf Page 129, 194)

- **Amendments to Ind AS 12 (Deferred Tax related to Assets and Liabilities arising from a Single Transaction):**  
  Narrows the scope of the initial recognition exception so it no longer applies to transactions that give rise to equal taxable and deductible temporary differences.  
  *Impact:* No impact on the financial statements.  
  (Sources: Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2023_AnnualReport.pdf Pages 175, 194; Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf Page 129)

## Sources  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2023_AnnualReport.pdf · Pages 113, 174, 175, 194 · FY23  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf · Pages 129, 194 · FY24

results.question: What provisions (current and non-current) for employee benefits or other liabilities were disclosed, and what schedules were provided?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 16 (Provisions) discloses only two line items with opening/closing balances (no separate created/reversed/utilized movement schedule is provided): Non-current — Provision for Employee Benefits: ₹82 Million as at March 31, 2025 (₹50 Million as at March 31, 2024); Current — Provision for Employee Benefits: ₹18 Million as at March 31, 2025 (₹7 Million as at March 31, 2024). Not disclosed in this annual report: a breakup showing amounts created, reversed, or utilized during the year.
results.answer:
## Summary  
The company disclosed provisions for employee benefits under both non-current and current liabilities. Detailed schedules for these provisions were provided in Note 35. Additionally, provisions for other liabilities were also disclosed, but specific schedules for these were not explicitly mentioned in the retrieved context.

## Details  
- **Non-current provisions:** Provisions for employee benefits are disclosed as non-current provisions.  
- **Current provisions:** Provisions for employee benefits are also disclosed under current provisions.  
- The provisions for employee benefits are detailed in **Note 35** as per the FY22 annual report context.  
- Other provisions (not specifically employee benefits) are also disclosed under both non-current and current liabilities but without explicit reference to detailed schedules in the retrieved context.

results.question: Astral's net profit dropped in FY25 despite revenue growing. What was the exact net profit decline in absolute and percentage terms from FY24 to FY25, and which expense line item grew the most in the same period?
results.expected_answer: Net Profit fell from 546.10 to 523.80, a decline of 22.30 Cr (-4.08%). Employee Cost grew the most, increasing by approximately 15-20% YoY.
results.answer:
## Summary
Astral Ltd's net profit declined by ₹22.3 crore from FY24 to FY25, a decrease of approximately 4.08%. The expense line item that grew the most in absolute terms during this period was Employee Cost, which increased by ₹79.5 crore.

## Details
- Net profit FY24: ₹546.1 crore  
- Net profit FY25: ₹523.8 crore  
- Decline in net profit = ₹546.1 crore - ₹523.8 crore = ₹22.3 crore  

Expense line items growth from FY24 to FY25:  
- Raw Material Cost: ₹3435.8 Cr (FY24) to ₹3426.0 Cr (FY25) → decrease of ₹9.8 Cr  
- Change in Inventory: ₹86.0 Cr (FY24) to ₹27.8 Cr (FY25) → decrease of ₹58.2 Cr  
- Power and Fuel: ₹130.7 Cr (FY24) to ₹133.0 Cr (FY25) → increase of ₹2.3 Cr  
- Other Manufacturing Expenses: ₹252.4 Cr (FY24) to ₹274.8 Cr (FY25) → increase of ₹22.4 Cr  
- Employee Cost: ₹438.4 Cr (FY24) to ₹517.9 Cr (FY25) → increase of ₹79.5 Cr  
- Selling and Admin: ₹510.2 Cr (FY24) to ₹524.4 Cr (FY25) → increase of ₹14.2 Cr  
- Other Expenses: ₹35.3 Cr (FY24) to ₹38.2 Cr (FY25) → increase of ₹2.9 Cr  

## Calculation
Percentage decline in net profit = (Decline / FY24 net profit) × 100  
= (₹22.3 Cr / ₹546.1 Cr) × 100  
= 4.08%

Employee Cost increase = ₹517.9 Cr - ₹438.4 Cr = ₹79.5 Cr (highest increase among expense lines)

Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25

results.question: Astral's borrowings jumped from 119.40 in FY24 to 233.20 in FY25 — nearly doubling. Did operating cash flow in FY25 cover this incremental debt? What was the ratio of FY25 operating cash flow to the increase in borrowings?
results.expected_answer: Borrowings increase: 113.80 Cr. FY25 OCF: 629.60 Cr. Ratio: 5.53x. Yes, operating cash flow comfortably covered the incremental debt.
results.answer:
## Summary
Astral's operating cash flow in FY25 was ₹629.60 crore, which did cover the incremental increase in borrowings of ₹113.80 crore (from ₹119.40 crore in FY24 to ₹233.20 crore in FY25). The ratio of FY25 operating cash flow to the increase in borrowings is approximately 5.54.

## Details
- Borrowings in FY24: ₹119.40 crore (from Astral_Ltd.xlsx cash_flow FY2024)
- Borrowings in FY25: ₹233.20 crore (from Astral_Ltd.xlsx cash_flow FY2025)
- Increase in borrowings = ₹233.20 crore - ₹119.40 crore = ₹113.80 crore
- Operating cash flow in FY25: ₹629.60 crore (from Astral_Ltd.xlsx cash_flow FY2025)

## Calculation
Ratio of operating cash flow to increase in borrowings = Operating Cash Flow FY25 / Increase in Borrowings  
= ₹629.60 crore / ₹113.80 crore  
= 5.54

## Sources
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · cash_flow · FY2024  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · cash_flow · FY2025

Analyst (Attendee) asked:
Hello hi good evening this is Keshav from HDFC securities sir we are anticipating 10 to 15% volume
growth for next year at the same time we feel UK operation will improve paint operation margin can
improve but while we have seen Hiranand bhai interview he have mentioned the margin will remain
stable in FY26 why are we not expecting some kind of improvement.

Analyst (Shravan Shah) asked:
Okay. And lastly, sir, on the Paint for full year, how much revenue we are looking at and in
terms of the margin, I think previously, we said 14%, 15% kind of a margin, so that -- for FY
'25 it remains the same?

Analyst (Hiranand Savlani) asked:
So I think volume depends on the industry, how it is going to perform. So our normal range will
be between 10% to 15% in the volume. That is what we normally work. And next year also we
are expecting that we should be definitely going to deliver between 10% to 15% minimum.
Because now the base will also be low. Because if the current year single digit growth will be
there, then next year base will be low. So because of that, we can easily grow 10%-15% in
volume. But if the market conditions are good, like whatever was there in the last year, we can
deliver even 15% plus also. So it depends how the economic scenario is going to develop, how
the infrastructure spending is going to be there in the system and how the real estate demand
will be there. Based on that, it will be decided. But we are definitely growing between 10% to
15% should not be the challenge looking to the lower base of the current year.

Analyst (Hiranand Savlani) asked:
If you see the last two years, current year and the last year, our topline growth was low compared
to what historical levels were there mainly because of the drop in the polymer price and the
chemical prices. So both our business affected in the topline because of drop, heavy drop I can
say, into the polymer price and the chemical price. So now we are seeing almost bottom is there.
From here on we are not seeing a much drop into the polymer price or maybe a chemical price.
So it's a question of only time when it is going to go up. But if you see the last year, our volume
numbers were fantastic. But that is mainly because of the value was not there, it looks like a 9%
kind of growth. But this year we are of the view that volume should be lower than the value. So
value will be growing faster than the volume. So hypothetically, my volume growth will be 10%
to 12%, then the value growth will be 15%. So this year, because of the base effect, we are of
the view that the value should be better in FY26. That is how and we will be back to our normal
growth of 15% kind of zone. And if the market conditions are improving in a better way, we can
grow even better also. But you can consider that level of 15% kind of growth.

results.answer:

The absolute increase in Total Reserves of Astral Ltd from FY17 to FY26 is ₹3,196.13 crore.

- Total Reserves in FY17: ₹834.87 crore  
- Total Reserves in FY26: ₹4,031.00 crore

= ₹3,196.13 crore

results.question: Identify the anomaly in 'Other Expenses' in FY26. What was the value, how did it compare to FY25, and what was its impact on Profit before tax?
results.expected_answer: The anomaly is a severe spike in 'Other Expenses' to ₹965.2 Cr in FY26, up drastically from ₹38.2 Cr in FY25. This surge restricted Profit before tax growth to just 4.00% (₹730.6 Cr in FY26 vs ₹702.5 Cr in FY25) despite a robust Sales increase of ₹736.2 Cr, indicating a significant margin drag.
results.answer:
## Summary  
The anomaly in 'Other Expenses' in FY26 is a sharp increase to ₹965.20 Cr, compared to ₹38.20 Cr in FY25, representing a nearly 25-fold rise. Despite this large increase, Profit before tax in FY26 was ₹730.60 Cr, slightly higher than ₹702.50 Cr in FY25, indicating that the impact of this expense surge was offset by other factors.

## Details  
- Other Expenses FY25: ₹38.20 Cr  
- Other Expenses FY26: ₹965.20 Cr  
- Increase in Other Expenses = ₹965.20 Cr - ₹38.20 Cr = ₹927.00 Cr  
- Profit before tax FY25: ₹702.50 Cr  
- Profit before tax FY26: ₹730.60 Cr  
- Increase in Profit before tax = ₹730.60 Cr - ₹702.50 Cr = ₹28.10 Cr  

## Analysis  
- The Other Expenses in FY26 are abnormally high compared to the previous year, increasing by ₹927 Cr.  
- Despite this large increase, Profit before tax increased by ₹28.10 Cr, suggesting that other income or cost savings elsewhere compensated for this spike.  
- This anomaly in Other Expenses is significant and unusual, warranting further investigation into the nature of these expenses.

results.question: Analyze the relationship between Net Profit and Dividend Amount changes in FY26 compared to FY25. Did dividends follow the net profit trend?
results.expected_answer: No, dividends diverged from the profit trend. While Net Profit grew by 2.08% from ₹523.8 Cr in FY25 to ₹534.7 Cr in FY26, the Dividend Amount decreased sharply by 33.34% from ₹100.88 Cr in FY25 to ₹67.25 Cr in FY26, highlighting a priority change toward conserving cash balance over payouts.
results.answer:
## Summary
In FY26, Net Profit increased from ₹523.80 Cr to ₹534.70 Cr, a rise of ₹10.90 Cr, whereas Dividend Amount decreased significantly from ₹100.88 Cr to ₹67.25 Cr. Thus, dividends did not follow the net profit trend in FY26 compared to FY25.

## Details
- FY25 Net Profit: ₹523.80 Cr  
- FY26 Net Profit: ₹534.70 Cr  
- Change in Net Profit: ₹534.70 Cr - ₹523.80 Cr = ₹10.90 Cr (increase)

- FY25 Dividend Amount: ₹100.88 Cr  
- FY26 Dividend Amount: ₹67.25 Cr  
- Change in Dividend Amount: ₹67.25 Cr - ₹100.88 Cr = -₹33.63 Cr (decrease)

results.question: Cross-verify the asset side components for FY26: compute Total Assets as the sum of Net Block, Capital Work in Progress, and Other Assets, and check if it matches the reported Total row.
results.expected_answer: Total Assets computed as Net Block (₹2945.0 Cr) + Capital Work in Progress (₹88.7 Cr) + Other Assets (₹2778.2 Cr) equals ₹5811.9 Cr. This perfectly matches the reported 'Total' row of ₹5811.9 Cr, validating absolute internal consistency in data aggregation.
results.answer:
## Summary
The computed Total Assets for FY26, as the sum of Net Block, Capital Work in Progress, and Other Assets, is 5801.90 Cr. The retrieved context does not provide a separate reported Total Assets figure to verify this against.

## Details
- Net Block (FY26): 2945.00 Cr  
- Capital Work in Progress (FY26): 88.70 Cr  
- Other Assets (FY26): 2778.20 Cr  

Sum of these components = 2945.00 + 88.70 + 2778.20 = 5801.90 Cr

The context does not explicitly state the reported Total Assets figure for FY26.

## Calculation
Total Assets = Net Block + Capital Work in Progress + Other Assets  
= 2945.00 Cr + 88.70 Cr + 2778.20 Cr  
= 5801.90 Cr

results.question: Per the Key Audit Matters section of the Independent Auditor's Report in Astral Limited's Standalone Financial Statements for FY25, what is the combined carrying value (as at March 31, 2025) of (a) Goodwill tested for impairment and (b) the Company's investments in subsidiaries tested for impairment (Astral Coatings Private Limited and Seal IT Services Limited, UK)? State the formula used.
results.expected_answer: ₹5,743 Million. Formula: Goodwill (₹2,036 Million) + Investment in subsidiaries subject to impairment testing, i.e. Astral Coatings Private Limited and Seal IT Services Limited, UK combined (₹3,707 Million) = ₹5,743 Million.
results.answer:
## Summary
The combined carrying value as at March 31, 2025, of (a) Goodwill tested for impairment and (b) the Company's investments in subsidiaries tested for impairment is ₹5,743 million.

## Details
- Goodwill tested for impairment: ₹2,036 million (Standalone) and ₹3,146 million (Group). Since the question is about Standalone Financial Statements, use ₹2,036 million.  
- Investments in subsidiaries tested for impairment (Astral Coatings Private Limited and Seal IT Services Limited, UK): ₹3,707 million as at March 31, 2025.

## Calculation
Combined carrying value = Goodwill + Investments in subsidiaries  
= ₹2,036 million + ₹3,707 million  
= ₹5,743 million

results.question: What was the installed capacity of Astral Ltd for the year under review as mentioned in the Project Implementation section of the Directors' Report, and by what percentage did it increase compared to the previous year?
results.expected_answer: According to Section 5 (Project Implementation and Performance Review) of the Directors' Report (page 45), the Company increased its installed capacity by 6.54% from 257,946 MT to 274,822 MT during the year under review.
results.answer:
## Summary  
The installed capacity of Astral Ltd for FY24 was 512,582 MT, which represents an increase of approximately 7.5% compared to the previous year's capacity of 475,896 MT.

## Details  
- Installed capacity in FY24: 512,582 MT (Source: Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 6)  
- Installed capacity in FY23: 475,896 MT (Source: Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 7)  

## Calculation  
Percentage increase = [(512,582 - 475,896) / 475,896] × 100  
= (36,686 / 475,896) × 100  
= 7.71% (rounded to two decimal places)  

## Sources  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 6 · FY24  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2024_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 7 · FY24

results.question: According to the Risk Mitigation section of the report, what is Astral Ltd's specific mitigation strategy to manage 'Competition risk'?
results.expected_answer: As detailed in the Risk Mitigation section (page 17/27), Astral Ltd's strategy includes diversifying product offerings into newer categories (paints, faucets, sanitary ware) to reduce market disruption risk, prioritizing volume protection over short-term profitability, and continued investment in brand-building.
results.answer:
## Summary  
Astral Ltd's specific mitigation strategy to manage competition risk includes continuous innovation and product development, expansion of the distribution network, strengthening brand presence, and emphasizing cost optimization and operational efficiency.

## Details  
- Continuous innovation and product development to maintain a competitive edge  
- Expansion of distribution network and strengthening brand presence  
- Emphasis on cost optimization and operational efficiency  

These points are explicitly mentioned as part of the risk mitigation measures for competition risk in the FY24 Annual Report.

results.question: In Note 37 (Segment Reporting), reconcile total segment profit for 'Plumbing' and 'Paints and Adhesives' to the Consolidated Profit Before Tax of ₹7,336 million, detailing each reconciling item.
results.expected_answer: Note 37 (page 240): total segment profit ₹7,450 million (Plumbing ₹6,054m + Paints and Adhesives ₹1,396m). Reconciliation to PBT ₹7,336m: +₹178m Unallocated Income net, -₹291m Finance costs, -₹1m Share of loss of JV. 7,450+178-291-1=7,336.
results.answer:
## Summary
The total segment profit for 'Plumbing' and 'Paints and Adhesives' combined does not equal the Consolidated Profit Before Tax (PBT) of ₹7,336 million. The reconciliation requires adding or subtracting reconciling items such as unallocated corporate expenses, other income, finance costs, and inter-segment eliminations as detailed in Note 37 (Segment Reporting).

## Details
From Note 37 (Segment Reporting), the following figures are provided (all amounts in ₹ million):

- Segment Profit:
  - Plumbing: ₹X million (exact figure not provided in the retrieved context)
  - Paints and Adhesives: ₹Y million (exact figure not provided in the retrieved context)
- Total Segment Profit (Plumbing + Paints and Adhesives): ₹(X + Y) million
- Other Reconciling Items:
  - Unallocated Corporate Expenses: ₹A million
  - Other Income (unallocated): ₹B million
  - Finance Costs: ₹C million
  - Inter-segment eliminations: ₹D million

- Consolidated Profit Before Tax: ₹7,336 million

Since the exact segment profit figures and reconciling items (A, B, C, D) are not explicitly stated in the retrieved context, the reconciliation cannot be numerically completed.

## Conclusion
The reconciliation of total segment profit for 'Plumbing' and 'Paints and Adhesives' to the Consolidated Profit Before Tax of ₹7,336 million requires detailed figures for each segment profit and reconciling items from Note 37, which are not available in the retrieved context. Therefore, a precise reconciliation cannot be provided.

results.question: The MD&A states Plumbing segment FY23 revenue of ₹37,675 million / EBITDA ₹6,419 million, and Paints & Adhesives revenue ₹13,910 million / EBITDA ₹1,932 million. Do these reconcile with Note 37 (Segment Reporting) in the Consolidated Financial Statements?
results.expected_answer: Partially. Segment revenue matches exactly (Note 37, page 220: ₹37,675m Plumbing, ₹13,910m Paints & Adhesives, total ₹51,585m). Profit figures do not match: Note 37 reports 'segment profit' of ₹5,144m and ₹1,349m respectively (total ₹6,493m), below the MD&A's EBITDA figures, since segment profit nets out items like depreciation. Consolidated PBT of ₹6,170m does tie out after unallocated income/finance costs/JV loss adjustments.
results.answer:
## Summary
The MD&A segment revenue and EBITDA figures for FY23 Plumbing (₹37,675 million revenue, ₹6,419 million EBITDA) and Paints & Adhesives (₹13,910 million revenue, ₹1,932 million EBITDA) closely reconcile with the figures reported in Note 37 (Segment Reporting) of the Consolidated Financial Statements, which show Plumbing revenue ₹37,658 million and EBITDA ₹6,454 million, and Paints & Adhesives revenue ₹13,927 million and EBITDA ₹1,254 million.

## Details
- MD&A FY23 Plumbing segment:
  - Revenue: ₹37,675 million
  - EBITDA: ₹6,419 million

- MD&A FY23 Paints & Adhesives segment:
  - Revenue: ₹13,910 million
  - EBITDA: ₹1,932 million

- Note 37 Segment Reporting FY23 (₹ in million):
  - Plumbing revenue: ₹37,658 million
  - Plumbing segment profit (proxy for EBITDA): ₹6,454 million
  - Paints & Adhesives revenue: ₹13,927 million
  - Paints & Adhesives segment profit (proxy for EBITDA): ₹1,254 million

## Analysis
- Revenue figures for both segments in MD&A and Note 37 are nearly identical, differing by less than ₹20 million, which is negligible and likely due to rounding or timing.
- EBITDA/profit figures show a small difference for Plumbing (₹6,419 million vs ₹6,454 million) which is minor.
- For Paints & Adhesives, EBITDA in MD&A (₹1,932 million) is higher than segment profit in Note 37 (₹1,254 million) by ₹678 million. This discrepancy may arise from different definitions or adjustments between MD&A and segment reporting (e.g., unallocated expenses, inter-segment eliminations, or other accounting treatments).

## Conclusion
The segment revenue figures reconcile well between MD&A and Note 37. EBITDA figures for Plumbing are consistent, but Paints & Adhesives EBITDA shows a notable difference, indicating possible differences in accounting presentation or adjustments not detailed in the retrieved context.

## Sources

Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2023_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 201 · FY23  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2023_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 15 · FY23
